news_summary: Cape Verde's surprising World Cup victory and insights into the psychology of penalty shoot-outs highlight inspiring performances and mental resilience under pressure.
news_summary_fr: La surprenante victoire du Cap-Vert à la Coupe du monde et les analyses sur la psychologie des tirs au but mettent en lumière des performances inspirantes et une grande résilience mentale face à la pression.
news_summary_es: La sorprendente victoria de Cabo Verde en el Mundial y las reflexiones sobre la psicología de las tandas de penaltis ponen de relieve unas actuaciones inspiradoras y la fortaleza mental bajo presión.

articles.title: How Cape Verde stunned World Cup to set up Argentina tie
articles.summary: How has a group of islands with just 525,000 inhabitants made it to the knockout rounds of the World Cup?
articles.content:
Cape Verde have made history by becoming the smallest nation to reach the knockout stage at the World Cup.

The prize for a side representing a group of 10 islands in the Atlantic Ocean will be to face defending champions Argentina in the last 32.

Players huddled around a mobile phone on the pitch following their goalless draw with Saudi Arabia to watch the final moments of Spain's win over Uruguay - a result that confirmed Cape Verde finished as Group H runners-up.

"Tears of pride and joy all around the stands," said BBC Radio 5 Live commentator Rob Law in Houston.

"There was a beautiful moment where they were all huddled around waiting on their phones, waiting for the full-time whistle to go.

"When it did, tears flowed on the pitch and in the stands as well. What a moment. The moment of the World Cup so far."

That followed an incredible goalless draw in their opening game against Spain, where 40-year-old goalkeeper Vozinha was the hero, and a 2-2 draw with two-time World Cup winners Uruguay.

"It's incredible what they are doing, it wasn't just one game against Spain, it is three games at the highest level," said Spain's former World Cup winner Juan Mata on ITV.

How has a nation of just 525,000 inhabitants, who qualified ahead of five-time African champions Cameroon, made it this far?
articles.reasoning: Cape Verde, a small nation with only 525,000 inhabitants, made history by becoming the smallest country to reach the knockout stage of the World Cup, demonstrating remarkable achievement and inspiring underdog success on a global sports stage. This has broad cultural and emotional significance, uplifting many beyond just sports fans.
articles.personality_title: Cape Verde becomes smallest nation to reach World Cup knockout stage
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cape Verde is a small island country in the Atlantic Ocean with just 525,000 people. It is not known as a big soccer nation and had never reached the knockout rounds in a World Cup before.

**What happened** – At the recent World Cup, Cape Verde surprised many by advancing from their group to the last 32 teams. They drew three games, including a goalless match against Saudi Arabia, a 2-2 draw with Uruguay, and a strong performance against Spain. Their qualification was confirmed when Spain beat Uruguay in the final group game.

**Impact** – This achievement makes Cape Verde the smallest country ever to reach the knockout stage of the World Cup. It shows how a small team with determination and teamwork can compete with some of the best teams in the world. The moment was emotional, with players and fans celebrating together, highlighting the pride the country feels.

**What's next step** – Cape Verde will now play against Argentina, the defending champions, in the last 32. This match will be a big challenge but also a chance for Cape Verde to gain more experience and recognition on the world stage.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Cape Verde’s historic World Cup performance proves that even small nations can achieve great success through teamwork and determination.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Cap-Vert devient le plus petit pays à atteindre les huitièmes de finale de la Coupe du Monde
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Cap-Vert est un petit pays insulaire dans l'océan Atlantique avec seulement 525 000 habitants. Ce pays n'était pas connu pour le football et n'avait jamais atteint les huitièmes de finale d'une Coupe du Monde.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Lors de la dernière Coupe du Monde, le Cap-Vert a surpris beaucoup de monde en se qualifiant pour les 32 meilleures équipes. Ils ont fait trois matchs nuls, dont un 0-0 contre l'Arabie Saoudite, un 2-2 contre l'Uruguay et une bonne performance contre l'Espagne. Leur qualification a été confirmée quand l'Espagne a battu l'Uruguay lors du dernier match de groupe.

**Impact** – Cette réussite fait du Cap-Vert le plus petit pays à atteindre les huitièmes de finale de la Coupe du Monde. Cela montre qu'une petite équipe, avec de la détermination et un bon travail d'équipe, peut rivaliser avec les meilleures équipes du monde. Ce moment a été très émouvant, avec des joueurs et des fans qui ont célébré ensemble, montrant la fierté du pays.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Cap-Vert va maintenant affronter l'Argentine, le champion en titre, en huitièmes de finale. Ce match sera un grand défi mais aussi une chance pour le Cap-Vert de gagner plus d'expérience et de reconnaissance.

**Phrase à retenir** – La performance historique du Cap-Vert en Coupe du Monde montre que même les petits pays peuvent réussir grâce au travail d'équipe et à la détermination.
articles.personality_title_es: Cabo Verde se convierte en la nación más pequeña en alcanzar la fase de eliminación directa del Mundial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cabo Verde es un pequeño país insular en el Océano Atlántico con solo 525,000 habitantes. No es conocido por su fútbol y nunca había llegado a la fase de eliminación directa en un Mundial.

**Qué pasó** – En el reciente Mundial, Cabo Verde sorprendió a muchos al avanzar de su grupo a los últimos 32 equipos. Empataron tres partidos, incluyendo un 0-0 contra Arabia Saudita, un 2-2 con Uruguay y una buena actuación contra España. Su clasificación se confirmó cuando España venció a Uruguay en el último partido del grupo.

**Impacto** – Este logro convierte a Cabo Verde en el país más pequeño en alcanzar la fase de eliminación directa del Mundial. Demuestra que un equipo pequeño con determinación y trabajo en equipo puede competir con los mejores del mundo. El momento fue emotivo, con jugadores y aficionados celebrando juntos, mostrando el orgullo del país.

**Próximo paso** – Cabo Verde ahora jugará contra Argentina, los actuales campeones, en los octavos de final. Este partido será un gran desafío pero también una oportunidad para que Cabo Verde gane más experiencia y reconocimiento.

**Frase para recordar** – El desempeño histórico de Cabo Verde en el Mundial prueba que incluso las naciones pequeñas pueden lograr grandes éxitos con trabajo en equipo y determinación.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a small, glowing island group shaped like Cape Verde in the vast Atlantic Ocean, with a radiant, protective shield symbolizing a goalkeeper’s gloves encircling it, surrounded by subtle silhouettes of a soccer ball and a mobile phone displaying a tiny glowing screen, all rendered in soft, natural blues, greens, and warm earth tones to evoke pride, unity, and historic achievement.

articles.title: I study World Cup penalty shoot-outs: they say a lot about the psychology of performance under pressure
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02043-0Football's most nail-biting feature can reveal ways for everyone to manage stressful situations better.
articles.content:
Sierra Leone’s John Thomas ‘Jay Tee’ Kamara scores during a penalty shoot-out against Azerbaijan in March 2026.Credit: Aziz Karimov/FIFA via Getty

Taking a penalty kick in a World Cup football shoot-out means performing an action under extreme pressure. The binary nature of the outcome, the importance of the shot for the game’s result, the drama surrounding penalties and the abundance of video evidence from every angle also make shoot-outs a fertile real-world laboratory for understanding human behaviour under pressure.

Lessons from penalty shoot-outs can reveal a lot about how people handle other stressful situations, such as important exams or job interviews.

I began researching penalty kicks systematically in 2004, shortly after earning my PhD in sport psychology. In a direct knock-out tournament (such as the later stages of the World Cup), teams resort to penalty shoot-outs to break a tie after extra time. My data stem from online archival records, video analyses and interviews with players and coaches. I have put my research into practice at the World Cup and Europe’s UEFA Champions League, helping football teams to prepare for the eventuality of penalties. But I have applied these insights to other sports, too. For instance, I was a pressure consultant for Europe’s golf team at the 2025 Ryder Cup. I advised on how to train for and cope with pressure at that year’s US venue.

One insight from the science of pressure in sport is that professional athletes are surprisingly similar to the rest of us: they, too, can be affected by the importance of the occasion. Top US basketball players miss more free throws at the end of a tight game than earlier on in the match, top golfers miss more putts when a large prize is on the line and top tennis players make more unforced errors towards the end of games, sets and matches than at the start.

A penalty shoot-out is similar. When pressure peaks, performance tends to drop. Footballers in a situation in which a missed shot would immediately cost their team the game are more likely to miss than are those who can fail with no direct consequences but can secure the win by scoring.

How do footballers cope? Historically, not so well. Coaches often admit that players haven’t trained much for penalty shoot-outs, and they don’t prepare a list of who will take the shots in advance. This collective escapism spreads to players, whose anxiety is intense. Penalty takers sometimes react so fast to the referee’s whistle that it seems as if they just want to get the shot over with.

But things are changing. Some teams in the 2026 World Cup started preparing for penalties years ago and have created rigorous structures and positive narratives around performance. England’s Football Association, for example, started its ‘penalty project’ in 2018 (for which I was consulted), abandoning the old ‘penalties are a lottery’ narrative and adopting a view that these shots are something that can be controlled with detailed analyses, careful preparation and quality training. This is how England’s team went from being the worst in the world at penalties (losing six out of seven shoot-outs between 1990 and 2012) to one of the best (winning three out of four since 2018).

Even if it is impossible to recreate the pressure of a major penalty shoot-out during training, such exercises are crucial. And experiencing some anxiety during training will help players to prepare for the real deal. Just like with vaccines, we can inoculate with low doses of pressure to effectively protect against high pressure later. In much the same way, students can benefit from rehearsing exams and job applicants should do mock interviews — with small rewards or consequences that raise the stakes.

But a more important insight is to avoid taking a penalty kick alone. Goalkeepers often try to break the penalty taker’s concentration, composure and confidence by throwing the ball away or by dancing along the goal line. The best penalty shooters do not just counter with individualized coping strategies — they rely on their teammates. In a shoot-out, everyone has a role. Each shot is a team event. The team’s goalkeeper secures a friendly introduction to the kick by handing the ball to the penalty taker. And an assigned buddy provides emotional support by escorting the player back after the event.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on research into penalty shoot-outs in football as a real-world study of human performance under pressure, revealing insights that can help people manage stress in various high-stakes situations like exams and job interviews. It highlights a positive shift in training approaches that improve outcomes and mental resilience, with broad applicability beyond sports.
articles.personality_title: How World Cup penalty shoot-outs teach us to handle pressure better
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In football, penalty shoot-outs happen when teams are tied after extra time, creating intense pressure because one shot can decide the game. These moments are studied by scientists to understand how people perform under stress.

**What happened** – Since 2004, a sport psychologist has studied penalty kick data, videos, and interviews to learn how players cope with pressure. This research helped teams at the World Cup and other sports events prepare better for high-pressure moments. For example, England’s football team improved their penalty shoot-out success by training carefully and changing their attitude about penalties.

**Impact** – The research shows even top athletes feel pressure and can make more mistakes in important moments. But with the right preparation and team support, players can perform better. This idea applies beyond sports—students and job seekers can practice stressful situations to improve their confidence and results.

**What's next step** – More teams and individuals are expected to use these training methods, focusing on teamwork and mental preparation. This can lead to better performance in sports and other areas where pressure matters, like exams or interviews.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Studying football penalty shoot-outs reveals how careful practice and teamwork help people manage pressure and perform better in stressful situations.
articles.personality_title_fr: Ce que les tirs au but de la Coupe du Monde nous apprennent pour mieux gérer la pression
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Lorsqu’un match de football est à égalité après la prolongation, une séance de tirs au but décide du vainqueur, créant une pression énorme car un seul tir peut changer le résultat. Ces moments sont étudiés pour comprendre comment les gens agissent sous stress.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Depuis 2004, un psychologue du sport analyse des données, vidéos et interviews pour savoir comment les joueurs gèrent cette pression. Ses recherches ont aidé des équipes à la Coupe du Monde et dans d’autres sports à mieux se préparer. Par exemple, l’équipe d’Angleterre a amélioré ses tirs au but grâce à un entraînement rigoureux et un changement d’attitude.

**Impact** – L’étude montre que même les meilleurs sportifs ressentent la pression et font plus d’erreurs dans les moments importants. Mais avec une bonne préparation et le soutien de l’équipe, ils peuvent mieux réussir. Cette idée s’applique aussi aux élèves et candidats qui peuvent s’entraîner pour mieux réussir leurs examens ou entretiens.

**Prochaine étape** – De plus en plus d’équipes et de personnes utiliseront ces méthodes, en misant sur le travail d’équipe et la préparation mentale. Cela pourrait améliorer les performances dans le sport et dans d’autres situations stressantes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Étudier les tirs au but montre que la pratique et le soutien d’équipe aident à mieux gérer la pression et à réussir dans les moments difficiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Lo que los penales en la Copa Mundial enseñan sobre cómo manejar la presión
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el fútbol, los penales deciden un partido empatado después del tiempo extra, creando una presión enorme porque un solo disparo puede definir el resultado. Estos momentos se estudian para entender cómo las personas actúan bajo estrés.

**Qué pasó** – Desde 2004, un psicólogo del deporte ha analizado datos, videos y entrevistas para aprender cómo los jugadores enfrentan la presión. Esta investigación ayudó a equipos en la Copa Mundial y otros deportes a prepararse mejor. Por ejemplo, el equipo de Inglaterra mejoró sus penales con entrenamiento cuidadoso y un cambio en su forma de pensar.

**Impacto** – El estudio muestra que incluso los mejores atletas sienten presión y cometen más errores en momentos importantes. Pero con preparación adecuada y apoyo del equipo, pueden rendir mejor. Esta idea también sirve para estudiantes y personas que se preparan para exámenes o entrevistas.

**Próximo paso** – Se espera que más equipos y personas usen estos métodos, enfocándose en el trabajo en equipo y la preparación mental. Esto puede mejorar el rendimiento en deportes y otras situaciones de presión.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Estudiar los penales revela que la práctica y el apoyo del equipo ayudan a manejar la presión y a rendir mejor en situaciones estresantes.
